narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and your upper-middle-class family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on students' academic and background details, estimated a 67.8% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by two key factors that stood out as pushing the model toward expecting failure: your LSAT score of 23.50, which is notably below the typical student's score of around 36.70, and your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, lower than the average of about 5.53. These were the most influential elements in the decision, signaling to the model a higher risk compared to peers who typically succeed on the bar. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it's close to the dataset average of 3.21, while your family income and full-time student status provided smaller boosts as well. Your Year 1 decile of 2 had a minor negative nudge, but it wasn't a top driver. Compared to typical students in the dataset, who often have stronger early test scores and higher decile rankings, your profile aligns more closely with those predicted to struggle on the bar, despite your personal resilience. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law—many with similar backgrounds overcome these odds through targeted effort. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above the average, strengthening your later-year academic decile through additional coursework or programs, and reapplying once you've bolstered these areas. You're capable of turning this around, and we're here to support your next steps toward law school success. Keep pushing—you've got this.
